rotary flight.def: Flight using rotating blades or wings to create lift.
rotary flight.comp: A type of heavier-than-air flight where lift is generated by rotating airfoils rather than fixed wings.
rotary flight.example: A helicopter achieves rotary flight with its spinning main rotor.

fixed-wing.def: Aircraft that use rigid wings and forward motion to create lift.
fixed-wing.comp: Airplanes are fixed-wing aircraft; they cannot hover without special thrust systems.
fixed-wing.example: An airliner is a fixed-wing aircraft.

rotor.def: A set of rotating blades that generates lift on a rotary-wing aircraft.
rotor.comp: The complete assembly of blades, hub, and mast that produces lift.
rotor.example: The main rotor sits on top of the helicopter.

rotor blade.def: A long, shaped wing that spins to create lift.
rotor blade.comp: Each individual airfoil attached to the rotor hub.
rotor blade.example: Helicopters usually have two to eight rotor blades.

lift.def: The upward force created by air moving over a wing or rotor blade.
lift.comp: One of the four forces of flight; opposes weight.

drag.def: Air resistance that opposes motion through the air.
drag.comp: One of the four forces of flight; opposes thrust.

thrust.def: The force that moves an aircraft forward or upward.
thrust.comp: One of the four forces of flight; opposes drag.
thrust.example: A helicopter rotor provides both lift and thrust.

weight.def: The downward pull of gravity on an aircraft.
weight.comp: One of the four forces of flight; opposes lift.
weight.example: To climb, lift must exceed weight.

torque.def: A twisting force. In helicopters, spinning the rotor one way tends to spin the body the other way.
torque.comp: Newton's third law reaction force requiring anti-torque systems.
torque.example: The tail rotor counters main rotor torque.

anti-torque.def: A system that prevents the helicopter body from spinning opposite to the rotor.
anti-torque.comp: Includes tail rotors, NOTAR, or coaxial counter-rotating rotors.
anti-torque.example: The tail rotor provides anti-torque force.

tail rotor.def: A small rotor on the tail that pushes against torque.
tail rotor.comp: Produces sideways thrust to counter main rotor torque and controls yaw.
tail rotor.example: Increasing tail rotor thrust yaws the nose.

collective.def: The control that changes all rotor blade angles together.
collective.comp: Increases or decreases total lift equally on all blades.
collective.example: Pulling up the collective makes the helicopter climb.

cyclic.def: The control that tilts the rotor disk to move in a direction.
cyclic.comp: Changes blade pitch selectively during rotation to tilt the rotor.
cyclic.example: Pushing the cyclic forward tilts the rotor and moves the helicopter forward.

yaw.def: Rotation around the vertical axis (nose left or right).
yaw.comp: Controlled by the tail rotor or anti-torque system.
yaw.example: Pressing the foot pedals changes yaw.

pitch.def: Rotation around the side-to-side axis (nose up or down).
pitch.comp: In helicopters, controlled by the cyclic stick.
pitch.example: Pulling back on the cyclic pitches the nose up.

roll.def: Rotation around the front-to-back axis (tilting side to side).
roll.comp: In helicopters, controlled by the cyclic stick sideways.
roll.example: Moving the cyclic left rolls the helicopter left.

vtol.def: Vertical Takeoff and Landing; ability to lift off without a runway.
vtol.comp: Key capability of helicopters and some tiltrotors.
vtol.example: A helicopter uses VTOL to take off from a rooftop.

hover.def: Remaining in one place in the air.
hover.comp: Requires lift to equal weight and control inputs to maintain position.
hover.example: Rescue helicopters hover over accident scenes.

autorotation.def: A safety descent where the rotor spins from upward airflow after engine failure.
autorotation.comp: Unpowered rotor rotation used for emergency landing.
autorotation.example: Pilots practice autorotation for engine-out emergencies.

autogyro.def: An aircraft with an unpowered rotor that spins from airflow, plus a propeller for thrust.
autogyro.comp: Not a helicopter; rotor is not powered for lift.
autogyro.example: Juan de la Cierva invented the autogyro in the 1920s.

tiltrotor.def: An aircraft with rotors that can tilt between vertical and horizontal positions.
tiltrotor.comp: Combines VTOL capability with airplane-like forward speed.
tiltrotor.example: The V-22 Osprey is a tiltrotor aircraft.

coaxial rotor.def: Two rotors mounted one above the other, spinning in opposite directions.
coaxial rotor.comp: Cancels torque without a tail rotor.
coaxial rotor.example: The Kamov Ka-50 uses coaxial rotors.

tandem rotor.def: Two large rotors, one at the front and one at the rear.
tandem rotor.comp: Cancels torque and provides heavy lift capability.
tandem rotor.example: The CH-47 Chinook has tandem rotors.

airfoil.def: A shaped surface designed to create lift from airflow.
airfoil.comp: Includes wings and rotor blades with curved upper surfaces.
airfoil.example: A rotor blade is a type of airfoil.

angle of attack.def: The angle between the blade and the oncoming air.
angle of attack.comp: Affects lift and drag; too high causes stall.
angle of attack.example: Increasing angle of attack increases lift until stall.

mast.def: The vertical shaft that supports the main rotor.
mast.comp: Connects the rotor hub to the transmission and airframe.
mast.example: The mast passes through the center of the rotor hub.

hub.def: The central part of the rotor that attaches the blades to the mast.
hub.comp: Holds blades and allows pitch changes.
hub.example: The hub connects all rotor blades to the mast.

nacelle.def: A housing on a tiltrotor that holds the engine and rotor.
nacelle.comp: Tilts to switch between vertical lift and forward thrust.
nacelle.example: The V-22 Osprey tilts its nacelles 90 degrees.

airflow.def: The movement of air over and around an aircraft.
airflow.comp: Essential for generating lift on any wing or rotor.
airflow.example: Rotor blades need airflow to create lift.

engine.def: A machine that converts fuel into mechanical power to spin the rotor.
engine.comp: Helicopter engines drive the transmission, which turns the rotor.
engine.example: A turboshaft engine is common in helicopters.

transmission.def: The gearbox that transfers power from the engine to the rotor.
transmission.comp: Changes engine speed to optimal rotor speed.
transmission.example: The transmission sits between the engine and the mast.

fuselage.def: The main body of the aircraft that holds crew, cargo, and systems.
fuselage.comp: The central structure to which all other parts attach.
fuselage.example: The fuselage houses the cockpit and passengers.

cockpit.def: The area where the pilot sits and controls the aircraft.
cockpit.comp: Contains flight controls, instruments, and displays.
cockpit.example: The cyclic and collective are in the cockpit.

blade pitch.def: The angle of a rotor blade relative to the oncoming air.
blade pitch.comp: Higher pitch increases lift and drag; lower pitch decreases them.
blade pitch.example: The collective changes blade pitch on all blades equally.

swashplate.def: A rotating mechanical device that transfers pilot control inputs to the spinning rotor blades.
swashplate.comp: Tilts and moves up/down to change blade pitch cyclically and collectively.
swashplate.example: The swashplate tilts when the pilot pushes the cyclic stick.

main rotor.def: The primary large rotor on top of a helicopter that generates lift.
main rotor.comp: Consists of blades, hub, and mast; provides lift and thrust.
main rotor.example: The main rotor spins at hundreds of RPM to keep the helicopter airborne.

rotor disk.def: The circular area swept by the spinning rotor blades.
rotor disk.comp: The disk tilts when cyclic input is applied, directing thrust.
rotor disk.example: Downwash flows through the rotor disk during hover.

downwash.def: The downward flow of air pushed by the rotor blades.
downwash.comp: Creates a column of accelerated air beneath the rotor disk.
downwash.example: Downwash can kick up dust and debris during a landing.

center of gravity.def: The point where an aircraft's weight is evenly balanced in all directions.
center of gravity.comp: Must stay within limits for stable flight; shifted by fuel and cargo.
center of gravity.example: A forward center of gravity makes the nose pitch down.

gyroscopic precession.def: A force applied to a spinning rotor takes effect 90 degrees later in the direction of rotation.
gyroscopic precession.comp: Explains why cyclic input affects the rotor disk opposite to where force is applied.
gyroscopic precession.example: Pushing the cyclic forward actually increases lift on the left side first.

translating tendency.def: The sideways drift caused by tail rotor thrust during hover.
translating tendency.comp: Also called hover drift; pilots correct it with cyclic input.
translating tendency.example: A helicopter hovers with a slight nose-left tilt to counter translating tendency.

dissymmetry of lift.def: The difference in lift between the advancing and retreating blades in forward flight.
dissymmetry of lift.comp: Advancing blade has higher airspeed and more lift; retreating blade has less.
dissymmetry of lift.example: Blade flapping compensates for dissymmetry of lift.

advancing blade.def: The rotor blade moving in the same direction as the aircraft's forward motion.
advancing blade.comp: Has higher relative airspeed and produces more lift than the retreating blade.
advancing blade.example: In forward flight, the advancing blade is on the right side of the rotor disk.

retreating blade.def: The rotor blade moving opposite to the aircraft's forward motion.
retreating blade.comp: Has lower relative airspeed and produces less lift; can stall at high speeds.
retreating blade.example: The retreating blade is on the left side during forward flight.

blade flapping.def: The upward and downward movement of rotor blades as they spin.
blade flapping.comp: Balances lift between advancing and retreating blades automatically.
blade flapping.example: The advancing blade flaps up, reducing its angle of attack and lift.

blade coning.def: The upward bending of rotor blades under lift load, forming a cone shape.
blade coning.comp: Caused by centrifugal force balancing lift; more lift means more coning.
blade coning.example: Heavy loads increase blade coning during takeoff.

stall.def: A sudden loss of lift when the angle of attack becomes too high.
stall.comp: Airflow separates from the blade surface, causing a drop in lift and increase in drag.
stall.example: A retreating blade can stall at high forward speeds.

induced drag.def: Drag created as a byproduct of generating lift.
induced drag.comp: Higher lift means higher induced drag; decreases with speed.
induced drag.example: Hovering produces more induced drag than forward flight.

parasitic drag.def: Drag caused by the aircraft's shape and external parts moving through air.
parasitic drag.comp: Includes form drag and skin friction; increases with the square of speed.
parasitic drag.example: Landing gear and antennas add parasitic drag.

relative wind.def: The airflow experienced by an airfoil as the aircraft moves.
relative wind.comp: Determines lift and drag; changes with aircraft speed and direction.
relative wind.example: The relative wind over the rotor blade affects lift.

tailwind.def: Wind blowing in the same direction as the aircraft is moving.
tailwind.comp: Increases ground speed but reduces relative wind over the airfoil.
tailwind.example: A tailwind reduces lift during takeoff.

headwind.def: Wind blowing opposite to the direction the aircraft is moving.
headwind.comp: Increases relative wind and lift; decreases ground speed.
headwind.example: Pilots prefer taking off into a headwind.

freewheeling unit.def: A clutch that lets the rotor spin freely if the engine fails.
freewheeling unit.comp: Allows the rotor to continue turning during autorotation.
freewheeling unit.example: The freewheeling unit disengages automatically during engine failure.

driven region.def: The part of the rotor disk where airflow drives the blade rotation.
driven region.comp: Located near the blade tips; produces drag that spins the rotor.
driven region.example: During autorotation, the driven region keeps the rotor spinning.

driving region.def: The part of the rotor disk that creates the most lift during autorotation.
driving region.comp: Located inboard on the blade; has a positive angle of attack.
driving region.example: The driving region sustains rotor RPM during a glide.

stalled region.def: The inner part of the rotor disk where the blade angle is too high and lift is lost.
stalled region.comp: During autorotation, this region creates drag but little lift.
stalled region.example: The stalled region is near the root of each blade in autorotation.

lead-lag.def: The forward and backward movement of a rotor blade in the plane of rotation.
lead-lag.comp: Also called hunting; compensates for changes in blade velocity.
lead-lag.example: Lead-lag hinges allow blades to move independently.

pitch link.def: A rod that connects the swashplate to the rotor blade, changing blade angle.
pitch link.comp: Pushes or pulls to increase or decrease blade pitch.
pitch link.example: When the swashplate tilts, pitch links twist the blades.

tail boom.def: The long rear structure connecting the main fuselage to the tail rotor.
tail boom.comp: Keeps the tail rotor far enough from the main rotor to be effective.
tail boom.example: The tail boom must be strong to handle tail rotor forces.

landing skids.def: Simple tubular frames that support the helicopter on the ground.
landing skids.comp: Lightweight and strong; absorb landing impact energy.
landing skids.example: Many training helicopters use landing skids instead of wheels.

horizontal stabilizer.def: Small wing-like surfaces on the tail that help keep the helicopter level.
horizontal stabilizer.comp: Provides pitch stability during fast forward flight.
horizontal stabilizer.example: The horizontal stabilizer counters nose-up tendencies at speed.

vertical fin.def: A fixed vertical surface on the tail that improves directional stability.
vertical fin.comp: Acts like a weather vane; helps keep the nose pointing forward.
vertical fin.example: The vertical fin adds stability during forward flight.

air intake.def: The opening that draws air into the engine for combustion.
air intake.comp: Must be kept clear of debris for proper engine performance.
air intake.example: Turbine engines need massive amounts of air through the intake.

exhaust.def: The system that expels hot gases from the engine.
exhaust.comp: Directs combustion gases safely away from the aircraft.
exhaust.example: Engine exhaust is extremely hot and must be vented upward.

turboshaft engine.def: A gas turbine engine optimized to produce shaft power rather than thrust.
turboshaft engine.comp: Common in helicopters; drives the transmission to spin the rotor.
turboshaft engine.example: Most modern helicopters use turboshaft engines.

stabilizer bar.def: A weighted bar mounted on the rotor hub that dampens blade movements.
stabilizer bar.comp: Acts as a mechanical gyroscope to improve hover stability.
stabilizer bar.example: The Bell 47 famously uses a stabilizer bar above the rotor.

NOTAR.def: NO TAil Rotor; a system that uses jet thrust instead of a tail rotor for anti-torque.
NOTAR.comp: Blows air through a slot in the tail boom for quieter, safer operation.
NOTAR.example: MD Helicopters uses NOTAR on some of their aircraft.

aerial screw.def: Leonardo da Vinci's 15th-century design for a vertical-lift machine.
aerial screw.comp: A helical airfoil meant to compress air and lift a platform.
aerial screw.example: Da Vinci's aerial screw was never built but inspired future designs.

single rotor.def: A helicopter configuration with one main rotor and a tail rotor.
single rotor.comp: The most common layout; requires anti-torque compensation.
single rotor.example: The Sikorsky R-4 was the first mass-produced single-rotor helicopter.

counter-rotating.def: Rotors that spin in opposite directions to cancel torque.
counter-rotating.comp: Used in coaxial and tandem rotor designs.
counter-rotating.example: Counter-rotating rotors eliminate the need for a tail rotor.

hovering.def: Maintaining a fixed position in the air with no forward movement.
hovering.comp: Requires continuous control adjustments to stay in place.
hovering.example: Helicopters hover to rescue people from rooftops.

forward flight.def: Movement of a helicopter in a horizontal direction.
forward flight.comp: Changes aerodynamics significantly compared to hover; introduces dissymmetry of lift.
forward flight.example: During forward flight, the advancing blade generates more lift.

unpowered rotor.def: A rotor that spins without engine power, driven by airflow.
unpowered rotor.comp: Used in autogyros and during helicopter autorotation.
unpowered rotor.example: An autogyro's unpowered rotor spins from forward motion.

rotor rpm.def: The rotational speed of the rotor measured in revolutions per minute.
rotor rpm.comp: Critical for maintaining lift; monitored constantly by the pilot.
rotor rpm.example: Low rotor RPM means less lift and possible settling.

turbulent flow.def: Chaotic, irregular airflow that reduces lift and increases drag.
turbulent flow.comp: Opposite of smooth laminar flow; occurs at high angles of attack.
turbulent flow.example: Stall causes turbulent flow over the blade.

attached flow.def: Smooth airflow that stays in contact with the airfoil surface.
attached flow.comp: Produces maximum lift and minimum drag.
attached flow.example: At low angles of attack, airflow remains attached to the blade.

flow separation.def: When airflow detaches from the airfoil surface, causing a stall.
flow separation.comp: Occurs when angle of attack exceeds the critical angle.
flow separation.example: Flow separation on the retreating blade can cause a stall.

Bernoulli's principle.def: The principle that faster-moving air has lower pressure.
Bernoulli's principle.comp: Explains how curved airfoils create lift: faster flow on top means lower pressure.
Bernoulli's principle.example: Bernoulli's principle helps explain rotor blade lift generation.

pressure difference.def: The difference in air pressure between the top and bottom of an airfoil.
pressure difference.comp: Creates the net upward force we call lift.
pressure difference.example: A rotor blade has lower pressure on top and higher pressure below.

kinetic energy.def: Energy possessed by a moving object.
kinetic energy.comp: In autorotation, the spinning rotor stores kinetic energy for landing.
kinetic energy.example: The pilot uses stored kinetic energy in the rotor to cushion landing.

phase lag.def: The 90-degree delay between cyclic input and rotor response due to gyroscopic precession.
phase lag.comp: Means maximum blade deflection occurs 90 degrees after maximum pitch change.
phase lag.example: Because of phase lag, cyclic inputs must be applied ahead of the desired direction.

thrust vector.def: The direction in which thrust is applied.
thrust vector.comp: In helicopters, tilting the rotor disk changes the thrust vector for directional flight.
thrust vector.example: Pushing the cyclic forward tilts the thrust vector forward.

longitudinal axis.def: An imaginary line running from the nose to the tail of the aircraft.
longitudinal axis.example: The longitudinal axis runs through the center of the fuselage.

lateral axis.def: An imaginary line running from wingtip to wingtip through the aircraft.
lateral axis.example: The lateral axis passes through the center of gravity.

vertical axis.def: An imaginary line running vertically through the center of the aircraft.
vertical axis.example: The vertical axis passes through the main rotor mast.

maneuverability.def: The ability of an aircraft to change its direction and attitude quickly.
maneuverability.comp: Helicopters trade stability for high maneuverability.
maneuverability.example: Helicopters can hover, fly sideways, and turn in place.

stability.def: The tendency of an aircraft to return to steady flight after a disturbance.
stability.comp: Helicopters are inherently unstable and require constant pilot input.

inherent instability.def: The natural tendency of a helicopter to drift or oscillate without control input.
inherent instability.comp: Requires active pilot control or autopilot to maintain attitude.
inherent instability.example: Without a stabilizer bar, early helicopters were very unstable.

pendulum effect.def: The swinging motion of the fuselage hanging beneath the rotor hub.
pendulum effect.comp: The center of gravity is below the rotor, creating a pendulum-like swing.
pendulum effect.example: The pendulum effect can cause oscillations during hover.

nose heavy.def: A condition where the center of gravity is forward of the ideal position.
nose heavy.comp: Causes the nose to pitch down; requires more aft cyclic to correct.
nose heavy.example: A full fuel tank in the nose can make the helicopter nose heavy.

heavy-lift.def: The capability to carry very large or heavy external loads.
heavy-lift.comp: Tandem rotor helicopters excel at heavy-lift operations.
heavy-lift.example: The CH-47 Chinook is a heavy-lift helicopter.

offshore oil rigs.def: Oil drilling platforms located in the ocean, often reached by helicopter.
offshore oil rigs.comp: Helicopters transport workers and supplies to rigs far from shore.
offshore oil rigs.example: Sikorsky S-92 helicopters serve offshore oil rigs worldwide.

surveillance.def: The close observation of an area or target from the air.
surveillance.comp: Police and military helicopters use surveillance for monitoring.
surveillance.example: A helicopter can hover overhead to provide continuous surveillance.

winches.def: Mechanical devices used to raise or lower people and cargo from a hovering helicopter.
winches.comp: Essential for rescue operations where landing is impossible.
winches.example: Rescue crews use winches to lower a paramedic to an injured hiker.

thermal cameras.def: Cameras that detect heat rather than visible light.
thermal cameras.comp: Help find people in darkness, smoke, or dense vegetation.
thermal cameras.example: Police helicopters use thermal cameras to track suspects at night.
